headline: Lewis and Clark Re-enter Montana via Lolo Pass, 1806
body:
The dawn of June 29, 1806, broke over the rugged Bitterroot Mountains, a landscape of profound geological artistry, where the Corps of Discovery, weary yet resolute, prepared to continue their homeward journey. Captain Meriwether Lewis, ever the keen observer of nature's grand design, noted the dispatch of Drewyer and R. Fields, their skilled hunters, to the warm springs, a natural marvel awaiting their arrival. The expedition, having traversed the formidable heights of the ridge for several days, now descended, bidding a silent farewell to the lingering snows that had clung to the upper reaches. They reached the main branch of the Kooskooske, a river of vibrant energy, its waters, thirty yards wide, rushing with a velocity that spoke of the mountains' untamed spirit. The riverbed, a mosaic of smooth, water-worn stones, bore witness to the ceaseless sculpting power of the currents, a testament to the patient work of ages.

Beyond this lively stream, the ascent began anew, a steep, two-mile climb that led them to a summit where the familiar trace of an old road emerged, a path now clearer, more deeply etched by the frequent passage of the Ootslashshoots, who journeyed from the valley of Clark's River to their ancestral fishing grounds. At midday, the expedition found respite at the quawmas flatts, a verdant expanse where their horses grazed contentedly after a twelve-mile march. It was here, amidst the tranquil beauty of the plain, that the discovery of two lost horses, one a packhorse laden with supplies and the other a riding horse belonging to Captain Lewis, necessitated the dispatch of J. Fields and Colter to retrieve them. The journey resumed, another seven miles leading them to the fabled warm springs, a place of natural wonder and restorative power.

Lewis, with his characteristic precision, described these springs, nestled at the base of a modest hill on the north side, near the bank of Travellers Rest Creek. The waters, emerging from the earth through the fissures of a grey freestone rock, formed a circular amphitheater of irregular, massive cliffs. Above the springs, a small, ten-acre quawmas plain unfolded, a testament to the land's fertile generosity. The principal spring, Lewis observed, possessed a temperature akin to the warmest baths of Virginia's renowned hot springs. He himself indulged, remaining in the bath for nineteen minutes, a duration that induced a profuse sweat, a cleansing of the body and spirit. Two adjacent springs, even hotter, caused the hand to smart upon immersion, their heat rivaling the most intense of Virginia's thermal waters. Both the men and their Native American companions found amusement and relief in the baths, engaging in a ritual of alternating between the scalding warmth and the icy chill of the creek, a stark contrast that invigorated the senses. Lewis, ever the naturalist, noted the sighting of a small black pheasant, the first he had encountered below the snow line, and observed young pheasants, mere days old, a vibrant sign of life's persistent renewal in this wild domain. He also noted the tracks of two barefooted Indians, whom he surmised were distressed refugees, fleeing from the Minnetares. The day's journey, a blend of arduous travel and profound natural discovery, concluded with the expedition encamped at these remarkable springs, their bodies soothed, their spirits refreshed, ready to face the continuing grandeur of the American wilderness. As Lewis penned in his journal, "I bathed and remained in 19 minutes, it was with dificulty I could remain thus long and it caused a profuse sweat."
